3: A type: 'Every place that the sole of your foot will tread upon I have given you.' Joshua leads the second generation of Israel into their inheritance — the land promise made to Abraham (Genesis 15:18-21), repeated to Isaac and Jacob, promised through Moses, now fulfilled through Joshua. Jesus is the greater Joshua: his name is the same, his mission is analogous (leading his people into the promised inheritance), and the rest he gives exceeds what Canaan could provide. Hebrews 4:1-11 carefully argues that the Canaan-rest was provisional: 'if Joshua had given them rest, God would not have spoken later of another day' (Joshua 4:8); the true rest is the sabbath-rest that remains for the people of God, entered by faith in Christ (Joshua 4:11).
1: The book opens after Moses's death — and here the name-typology strikes first. Yehoshua (Joshua) means 'YHWH saves,' the precise Hebrew form from which Iesous (Jesus) derives. The author of Hebrews makes the identification explicit: 'If Joshua (Iesous) had given them rest, God would not have spoken of another day' (Hebrews 4:8). The Joshua who replaces Moses after his death is a deliberate type: as Moses (the Law) cannot bring the people into the final rest, so Joshua (the name of Jesus) leads them in. The entire book of Joshua is structured around this substitution — the Law leads to the border; only Jesus brings into the inheritance.
2: 'Now therefore arise, go over this Jordan, you and all this people, into the land' — the Jordan crossing as baptism typology is anchored here at the command itself. Paul draws the Exodus-Jordan parallel in 1 Corinthians 10:1-2: 'all were baptized into Moses in the cloud and in the sea.' Origen extended this to the Jordan: the crossing from Transjordan into Canaan = passing through baptism into the promised inheritance. The command to cross carries Christological weight: Jesus himself was baptized in the Jordan (Matthew 3:13-17), and his baptism opened the new era of conquest over sin and death. As Joshua's commission began at the Jordan, so Jesus's public ministry began at the Jordan.
4: The territorial extent of the promise — from the wilderness to Lebanon, from the Euphrates to the Great Sea — reflects the maximal reach of Israel's inheritance never fully realized under Joshua. The type presses toward anti-type: Christ's inheritance is not a strip of Levantine territory but the whole earth ('Ask of me, and I will make the nations your heritage, and the ends of the earth your possession,' Psalms 2:8). Joshua's partial realization of a bounded land promise is the shadow; Christ's universal inheritance is the substance. The 'land flowing with milk and honey' that Joshua would enter is the prototype of the new creation that Christ inherits and shares with his people (Hebrews 11:10,16; Revelation 21:1-4).
5: 'No man shall be able to stand before you all the days of your life... I will not leave you or forsake you' — Hebrews 13:5 quotes this promise directly and applies it to every believer in Christ: 'For he has said, "I will never leave you nor forsake you."' The promise given to Joshua is fulfilled in Christ and extended to all who are in Christ. Jesus's own promise echoes it verbatim: 'I am with you always, to the end of the age' (Matthew 28:20). YHWH's personal presence with Joshua — the basis of every military victory — types the indwelling of the Spirit in believers, the presence that makes conquest of sin possible and guarantees the ultimate inheritance.
6: 'Be strong and courageous, for you shall cause this people to inherit the land' — the commissioning formula given to Joshua is echoed in Paul's exhortation to Timothy ('Be strengthened by the grace that is in Christ Jesus,' 2 Timothy 2:1) and to all believers ('Be strong in the Lord and in the strength of his might,' Ephesians 6:10). The phrase bridges Joshua's military commission and the spiritual warfare of the NT: the source of strength is the same divine presence, now mediated through Christ. Joshua's commission was for territorial conquest; Christ's commission to his disciples expands this to cosmic scope — 'make disciples of all nations' (Matthew 28:19) — but the enabling presence is identical.
7: 'Only be strong and very courageous, being careful to do according to all the law that Moses my servant commanded you' — Christ himself embodied this complete Torah-faithfulness: 'Do not think that I have come to abolish the Law or the Prophets; I have not come to abolish them but to fulfill them' (Matthew 5:17). Joshua's success was conditioned on not turning 'to the right hand or to the left' from the Torah — Christ fulfilled this condition perfectly where every human failed. His 'not turning aside' from the Father's will through Gethsemane to the cross is the ultimate Joshua-type faithfulness: the conquest accomplished through absolute obedience to the divine command.
8: 'This Book of the Law shall not depart from your mouth, and you shall meditate on it day and night' — the Word that Joshua was to meditate on was the written Torah; Christ is the Word incarnate (John 1:14). The meditation-command types the union of Christ with the Father's word: 'the words that I say to you I do not speak on my own authority, but the Father who dwells in me does his works' (John 14:10). The promise 'you will have good success' (tatzliach) is fulfilled in Christ through his resurrection — the ultimate vindication of the one who did not let the Word depart from his mouth. Those united to Christ now meditate on the word 'in him' (Colossians 3:16).
9: 'Do not be frightened, and do not be dismayed, for the LORD your God is with you wherever you go' — Jesus speaks virtually this same word at the great commission: 'Fear not' is his characteristic word of resurrection courage (Matthew 28:10; Revelation 1:17). The basis of Joshua's fearlessness — divine presence — is the same basis of Christian fearlessness: 'I am with you always' (Matthew 28:20). The disciples of Jesus face a conquest mission that is genuinely terrifying (Matthew 10:16-39) and need exactly this reassurance. Christ does not send his people where he himself will not go — his presence is the conquest's guarantee, as it was for Joshua.
10: Joshua's command to the officers to prepare for the crossing within three days carries a resonance: it was after three days that the miracle of new creation occurred — the Jordan parted and Israel entered the land. The three-day preparation is a shadow of the three days of Christ's death and resurrection, after which the new humanity entered the new creation. As Israel's officers organized the crossing that would happen in three days, the disciples waited three days for the crossing that would change everything. The three-day cadence runs through the OT as a resurrection pattern (Jonah 1:17; Hosea 6:2; Genesis 22:4) before its definitive fulfillment in Matthew 12:40.
11: 'Prepare provisions for yourselves, for within three days you are to pass over this Jordan to go in to take possession of the land' — the command to prepare food for the crossing echoes the Last Supper, where Jesus prepared his disciples for the crossing through death into the new creation. 'Do this in remembrance of me' (Luke 22:19) is the provision for the journey through the Jordan of death and resurrection. The manna ceased when Israel ate the produce of the land (Joshua 5:12); the Eucharist as 'food for the crossing' will be replaced by the marriage supper of the Lamb when the ultimate land is entered (Revelation 19:9).
12: The Transjordanian tribes — Reuben, Gad, and half-Manasseh — were already settled but were obligated to cross and fight for their brothers before returning to their inheritance. This types the solidarity Christ showed in the incarnation: already possessing his eternal inheritance in the Father's presence, he 'crossed over' into our condition to fight for those who had not yet received their inheritance (Philippians 2:5-8; Hebrews 2:14-15). As the settled tribes did not excuse themselves from their brothers' warfare, Christ did not excuse himself from humanity's warfare against sin and death. His incarnation is the crossing-over of the one who had every reason to remain on the far side.
13: 'Remember the word that Moses the servant of the LORD commanded you: The LORD your God is providing you a place of rest' — the rest-promise (Hebrew menucha) carries forward into the NT's climactic rest theology. Hebrews 3-4 expounds this passage directly: the rest Joshua gave was a type pointing to the 'sabbath rest' that remains for the people of God (Hebrews 4:9). 'For whoever has entered God's rest has also rested from his works as God did from his' (Hebrews 4:10). The Transjordanian tribes' settled rest types believers who are already seated with Christ in the heavenly places (Ephesians 2:6) while still fighting the earthly battle.
14: The armed warriors crossing the Jordan to fight for the inheritance of those not yet settled types Christ's atoning work on behalf of those who could not win their own inheritance. 'While we were still sinners, Christ died for us' (Romans 5:8) — he crossed through death for the sake of those who lacked the righteousness to cross themselves. The image of the Transjordanian warriors going before their brothers 'armed' while the women and children remained is a picture of Christ as the warrior-champion who fights the decisive battle in the believer's place (Isaiah 59:16; Joshua 63:3-5). The 40,000 armed men crossing ahead of Israel type Christ as the forerunner who opens the way.
15: 'Until the LORD gives rest to your brothers as he has to you, and they also take possession of the land' — the promise of rest for all the tribes is an eschatological vision: not one tribe left behind, not one member of the covenant community excluded from the inheritance. Christ's work has this same totalizing scope: 'I, when I am lifted up from the earth, will draw all people to myself' (John 12:32). The Transjordanian tribes will receive their full rest only when all Israel has received its rest — Christ's own 'rest' in his resurrection glory is complete only when the full number of his people is gathered (Revelation 6:11).
16: The elders' covenant-loyalty declaration — 'All that you have commanded us we will do, and wherever you send us we will go' — types the covenant response that Christ secured for his people. What Israel promised here but failed to sustain, Christ accomplished in his own perfect obedience: 'My food is to do the will of him who sent me and to accomplish his work' (John 4:34). Moreover, the Spirit of Christ given at Pentecost creates this covenant responsiveness in believers: 'I will put my Spirit within you, and cause you to walk in my statutes' (Ezekiel 36:27). The obedience Israel pledged in words, Christ fulfilled in actuality, and the Spirit reproduces in the new covenant community.
17: 'Just as we obeyed Moses in all things, so we will obey you' — the transfer of covenant loyalty from Moses to Joshua types the transfer from Torah to Christ. Paul's argument in Galatians and Romans is precisely this: the Mosaic administration has served its purpose and given way to the Joshua-administration. 'For Christ is the end of the law for righteousness to everyone who believes' (Romans 10:4). Those in Christ have transferred their primary covenant allegiance from Moses (the written code) to Jesus (the living Lord). The death penalty for rebellion against Joshua's command (Joshua 1:18) anticipates the gravity of rejecting Christ: 'How shall we escape if we neglect such a great salvation?' (Hebrews 2:3).
18: 'Only be strong and courageous' — the people's charge to Joshua mirrors YHWH's earlier words back to their leader, and the phrase closes Joshua 1 as a bookend of divine-and-human encouragement. For Christ, the Father's voice at his baptism ('This is my beloved Son, with whom I am well pleased,' Matthew 3:17) is the divine encouragement that sends him into his conquest. The human confirmation came at the transfiguration (Matthew 17:5) and in John 12:28 ('Father, glorify your name — and a voice came from heaven: I have glorified it, and I will glorify it again'). Christ's Joshua-commission was ratified both from above and from the human response of those who recognized his divine authority.
